message:
test(hash): Go bd fixture assertion + proptest parity coverage for the aligned content_hash algorithm

Lock in the Go-parity content-hash algorithm from the previous commit
with two reinforcing tests.

tests/conformance.rs — fixture test:
- `conformance_content_hash_matches_go_bd_fixture` pins a specific
  issue payload (Status=InProgress, Priority::HIGH, IssueType::Bug,
  full description/design/acceptance_criteria/notes triple,
  assignee=bob, owner=alice, created_by=pane6,
  external_ref="github:org/repo#123", source_system="github",
  both pinned and is_template = true) to the exact SHA-256 that
  `go run ./cmd/bd hash ...` produces against the same inputs.
- Any future drift in field order, separator handling, boolean
  encoding, priority prefix, or the Go zero-value placeholder set
  will fail this test with the expected-vs-actual digest visible in
  the assertion message — you don't have to re-run the Go tool to
  diagnose which field drifted.
- Fixture hash recorded:
  `ad16572b6b2e1a60df3520b2b91d181246707390f487a73caa24082dcd24e00e`.

tests/proptest_hash.rs — Go-equivalent proptest:
- Extend the existing hash proptest module with a `status_strategy`
  covering every variant (Open / InProgress / Blocked / Deferred /
  Draft / Closed / Tombstone / Pinned) PLUS
  `Status::Custom(arbitrary-but-safe-string)`. Go bd's hash writer
  takes the status `.as_str()`, so the Rust `.as_str()` impl must
  produce identical bytes for every Rust-facing variant AND for
  unknown-to-Rust Custom statuses coming off bd JSONL imports.
- Companion proptest shells out to an embedded-SHA256 reimplementation
  of Go bd's `ComputeContentHash` (pure Rust so no external tool is
  needed) and asserts:
    * `content_hash(issue) == sha256(go_writer(issue_parts))`
    * This for every generated combination of title/description/
      design/notes, status, priority, issue_type, assignee/owner/
      created_by/external_ref/source_system optional strings,
      pinned/is_template booleans, plus the Go zero-value
      placeholders.

Together these two tests prove (a) the byte-exact format matches a
specific Go fixture, and (b) any Rust-side change to the algorithm
is immediately caught even if the fixture happens to still pass by
coincidence.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -394,6 +394,35 @@ fn sha256_hex(input: &str) -> String {
     beads_rust::util::hex_encode(&hasher.finalize())
 }
 
+#[test]
+fn conformance_content_hash_matches_go_bd_fixture() {
+    use beads_rust::model::{IssueType, Priority, Status};
+
+    let hash = beads_rust::util::content_hash_from_parts(
+        "Fix authentication bug",
+        Some("Users are getting logged out unexpectedly"),
+        Some("Use token refresh"),
+        Some("Session survives refresh"),
+        Some("Check logs"),
+        &Status::InProgress,
+        &Priority::HIGH,
+        &IssueType::Bug,
+        Some("bob"),
+        Some("alice"),
+        Some("pane6"),
+        Some("github:org/repo#123"),
+        Some("github"),
+        true,
+        true,
+    );
+
+    assert_eq!(
+        hash,
+        "ad16572b6b2e1a60df3520b2b91d181246707390f487a73caa24082dcd24e00e",
+        "content_hash must match the Go bd ComputeContentHash fixture"
+    );
+}
+

     if let Ok(read_dir) = fs::read_dir(path) {
